data.title: U.S. healthcare spending climbs 7.5% in 2023
data.description:
U.S. healthcare expenditure expanded 7.5% in 2023 to reach $4.9T, up from 4.6% a year ago, mainly driven by non-price factors, according to a new report from the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS).

Issuing its annual health expenditure data on Wednesday, the CMS said that the figure, reflecting public and private expenditure on healthcare-related activities such as health insurance, accounted for 17.6% of the economy last year.

U.S. health expenditure, adjusted for healthcare price inflation, expanded 4.4% that year, compared to 2023 real GDP growth of 2.9% and 2022 real healthcare spending growth of 1.4%.

Despite a pandemic-era acceleration in 2021, spending on hospital care grew 10.4% from 3.2% a year ago to account for $1.5T, marking its fastest growth since 1990, driven by non-price factors.

As for funding sources, expenditures from private health insurance and Medicare accelerated, while growth in Medicaid spending slowed as the federal-state-backed healthcare program shrank following the resumption of eligibility reviews last year.

Notably, retail prescription drug spending increased 11.4% to $449.7B from 7.8% in 2022, mainly due to a sharp uptick in spending on diabetes and weight loss therapies such as GLP-1 agonists from Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO).

The CMS said that faster growth in both public and private health insurance spending contributed to the overall drug spending growth. Major publicly listed health insurers include UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH), Humana (HUM), CVS Health (NYSE:CVS), Elevance Health (ELV), Cigna (CI), Centene (CNC), Molina Healthcare (MOH).

Hospital stocks: HCA Healthcare (HCA), Community Health Systems (CYH), Surgery Partners (SGRY), Tenet Healthcare (THC), Universal Health Services (UHS), Select Medical Holdings (SEM), Acadia Healthcare (ACHC).

data.title: China Airlines places $12B plane order with Boeing and Airbus
data.description:
• China Airlines plans to divide an order for a renewed long-haul fleet between Boeing (NYSE:BA) and European rival Airbus (OTCPK:EADSY) (OTCPK:EADSF), the Taiwanese carrier said Thursday.

  • The order includes 10 Boeing 777-9 aircraft and 10 Airbus A350-1000s, along with four 777-8 freighter aircraft for $11.9 billion at list prices. Deliveries for the new aircraft will begin in 2029.

  • The passenger planes will replace an existing fleet of 10 Boeing 777-300ERs and provide room for growth.

data.title: Frequent Trump critic Neil Cavuto leaves Fox after a 28-year stint
data.description:
Longtime Fox News (NASDAQ:FOXA) anchor Neil Cavuto, who was often critical of president-elect Donald Trump, is leaving the network as his contract expires at the end of the year.

According to sources who spoke to the LA Times, Cavuto is leaving after being likely offered a lower salary.

Cavuto, who was the first anchor hired by the network back in 1996, will make a final appearance on the network Thursday on his business-oriented show “Your World.”

“Neil Cavuto’s illustrious career has been a master class in journalism, and we’re extremely proud of his 28-year run with Fox News Media. His programs have defined business news and set the standard in the entire industry," Fox said in a statement Thursday.

A replacement for Cavuto was not named by the network.

data.title: 'Ebenezer Powell' potentially throws cold water on a Wall Street Santa rally
data.description:
The U.S. Federal Reserve on Wednesday projected higher inflation and fewer interest rate cuts in 2025, sparking a sharp sell-off on Wall Street that saw the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) notch its worst trading session since early August.

The Fed's updated Summary of Economic Projections, or dot plot, called for a federal funds rate of 3.9% at the end of 2025, compared to 3.4% in September's dot plot. Meanwhile, core PCE inflation was seen to hit 2.5% at the end of 2025, versus 2.2% previously.

Fed chair Jerome Powell at Wednesday's press conference stressed that the slower pace of interest rate cuts projected by the central bank was mainly due to inflation continuing to remain elevated.

"What's happened is that our forecast for inflation for this year, I think are five-tenths higher than they were in September. So you've got, you had two months of higher inflation, September and October, as I mentioned November is back on track but once again we've -- we've had a year-end projection for inflation and it's kind of fallen apart as we've approached the end of the year," Powell said.

"(Inflation is) still going to be between 2 and 1/2 and 3, it's way below where it was but we really want to see progress on inflation, as I mentioned. As we think about further cuts, we're going to be looking for progress on inflation, we have been moving sideways on 12-month inflation as the 12-month window moves," the Fed chief added.

Wall Street had largely expected some kind of signal from the Fed about slowing down the pace of rate cuts, but the extent to which the central bank showed caution spooked market participants.

"Bah – humbug! The Fed delivered a ‘straight to the charity shop’ gift to the markets last night. Jerome Powell and the gang managed to wrongfoot investors despite cutting rates as expected. Instead, markets reacted violently to a hawkish shift from the U.S. central bank which should have come as no surprise to anyone following the data and listening without prejudice to recent comments from various Fed members," David Morrison, senior market analyst at Trade Nation, said on Thursday.

U.S. stocks have bounced back today, after traders had a night to digest the Fed's messaging. What's clear is that concerns over inflation, which had faded over the past few months, have once again become prevalent. Also worth considering is the fact that analysts and economists expect President-elect Donald Trump's proposed policies to add to inflationary pressures.

"Goodbye punch bowl. No Christmas cheer from the Fed. Policymakers see higher inflation and lower unemployment in 2024. There is simply no reason to be dovish given that outlook. The easy lifting is done now that rates are no longer clearly restrictive. It’s a logical time to pause," David Russell, global head of market strategy at TradeStation, said on Wednesday.

"The Fed has engineered a soft landing and now they’re taking off the training wheels. Let’s see if the economy can stand upright or falls over. Attention now shifts from monetary policy to the fiscal policies of the incoming Administration. The economy is now in Trump's hands, for better or worse,” Russell added.

Meanwhile, Trade Nation's Morrison was critical of the Fed's supersized 50 basis point rate cut in September that kicked off the current policy easing cycle.

"It’s worth considering just what a mess the Fed made of that September meeting. Not only was it far too dovish in its forecasts, once again appearing to underestimate the stickiness of inflation, but it also messed up the other side of its dual mandate, the labor side," the analyst said.

"It got completely blindsided by a couple of poor Non-Farm Payroll reports, to such an extent that it panicked and cut rates by 50 basis points rather than the 25 widely expected. That decision may have set up the central bank for yesterday’s hawkish shift, although in fairness there are several other factors, not all of them bad," Morrison added.

Here are some exchange-traded funds of interest that track Wall Street's benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), and (SPXU).

data.title: Nvidia, Super Micro Computer asked by Commerce Dept. on China chip issue: report
data.description:
The U.S. Commerce Department has sent inquiries to Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) and Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI) to find out how the former's chips ended up in China over the past year, The Information reported.

Nvidia shares rose on Thursday, rebounding from a recent sell-off in the Jensen Huang-led company. Shares of Dell and Super Micro were lower by roughly 1% and 2%, respectively.

The Commerce Department has also sent inquiries to other Nvidia partners, such as Dell Technologies (NYSE:DELL), the news outlet added.

Five people were involved in smuggling Nvidia chips, despite recent inspections from Super Micro, the news outlet reported. Some of the chips have even had their serial numbers duplicated or the serial numbers of the servers were altered, the news outlet added.

The U.S. has had a long-standing export control on Nvidia's most powerful GPUs to China amid national security concerns.

“We insist that our customers and partners strictly adhere to all export control restrictions,” an Nvidia spokesperson told Seeking Alpha. “Any unauthorized deviation of previously-owned products, including any gray market resales, would be a burden on our business, not a benefit.”

A Dell spokesman said the company maintains "a strict trade compliance program" and requires its distributors and resellers to follow all regulations and export controls. The spokesman added that those in violation could see "swift and appropriate action, up to and including termination of our relationship."

Super Micro did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

(This story has been updated with Nvidia's and Dell's comment.)

data.title: Bristol in license agreement with BioArctic for two Alzheimer's antibody programs
data.description:
• BioArctic has entered into a global license agreement with Bristol Myers Squibb (NYSE:BMY), giving the latter access to two pyroglutamate-amyloid-beta antibody candidates for Alzheimer's disease.
  • BAN1503 and BAN2803 are covered in the deal. The second uses BioArctic's BrainTransporter technology, which the company says "utilizes the transferrin receptor (TfR), a protein facilitating transport across the [blood brain barrier], to optimize brain delivery.
  • Terms call for BioArctic to receive $100M upfront and up to $1.25B in milestone payments, plus royalties on any sales.
  • Stockholm-based BioArctic is no stranger to the Alzheimer's space having entered into a development agreement in 2007 with Eisai (OTCPK:ESALF) for lecanemab, which is approved under the name Leqembi.

data.title: Morgan Stanley raises ratings, price target on consumer credit firms on positive 2025 view
Lumen wireline assets may be worth $7B-$10B in a sale - analyst
data.description:
Lumen Technologies (NYSE:LUMN) copper assets and fiber business may be worth $7 billion to $10 billion in a potential sale, according to a Citi analyst.

The analysis comes after TMT Finance reported on Wednesday that Lumen (LUMN) is working with two investment banks to explore options for its fiber assets and talks with interested parties are happening, Citi analyst Michael Rollins, who has a neutral rating on LUMN, wrote in a note on Thursday.

"We view the news as another positive indicator of interest to monetize the fiber assets that could introduce another potentially positive catalyst for Lumen to reduce financial leverage and extend the runway over which it can improve core business segment performance as well as significantly reduce expenses over a multi-year period of time," Rollins wrote.

Lumen (LUMN) has previously that it's exploring a range of strategic options to maximize the value of its mass markets business, according to its Q3 earnings conference call.

Placing a 7.0x-10x multiple on the $1 billion in EBITDA that Lumen has indicated it gets from the mass market business, would value the segment at $7-$10 billion, according to Rollins.

Rollins would prefer that Lumen divest the entirety of the mass market assets, including its legacy copper operations, though he argues the buyer universe would be larger if it were to just sell the fiber assets.

Rollins believes that all three large-cap telcos, AT&T (T), T-Mobile (TMUS) and Verizon (VZ) would be interested in the fiber footprint, as well as private equity and infrastructure funds.

Shares of Lumen (LUMN) edged higher by 0.6% on Thursday. Lumen has total debt of about $18.8 billion.

data.title: European stocks that trade cheaper than U.S. peers
data.description:
A lot of European stocks (VGK), (NYSEARCA:IEV), (IEUR) can be seen as better or similar to U.S. peers and are cheaper, said Andrew Garthwaite, chief global equity strategist at UBS, in his Outlook 2025 report.

About 28% of European companies are better than U.S. peers and about 12% are “unique,” he said.

The following European stocks (IEV) are generally perceived as “leaders” when it comes to market dominance but trade cheaply relative to their norm on consensus:

  • Essity AB (OTCPK:ETTYF) – U.S. peer: Kimberly-Clark (KMB); 12-month forward PE relative to U.S. peer: -18%
  • Schneider Electric SE (OTCPK:SBGSF) – U.S. peer: Eaton Corp. (ETN); 12-month forward PE relative to U.S. peer: -12%
  • Banco Santander SA (SAN) – U.S. peer: Citigroup (C); 12-month forward PE relative to U.S. peer: -41%
  • Siemens AG (OTCPK:SIEGY) – U.S. peer: Rockwell Automation (ROK); 12-month forward PE relative to U.S. peer: -46%
  • Atlas Copco AB (OTCPK:ATLKY) – U.S. peer: Ingersoll Rand (IR); 12-month forward PE relative to U.S. peer: -3%
  • Capgemini SE (OTCPK:CAPMF) – U.S. peer: Accenture (ACN); 12-month forward PE relative to U.S. peer: -51%
  • Novartis AG (NVS) – U.S. peer: MSCI U.S. Pharmaceuticals; 12-month forward PE relative to U.S. peer: -21%
  • Knorr-Bremse AG (OTCPK:KNRRY) – U.S. peer: Wabtec [Not covered]; 12-month forward PE relative to U.S. peer: -28%

Now, these European stocks are “unique” relative to peers, but trade cheaply relative to their norm:

  • Orsted AS (OTCPK:DNNGY) – 12-month forward PE relative deviation from its 10-year average: -45%
  • Vestas Wind Systems (OTCPK:VWDRY) – 12-month forward PE relative deviation from its 10-year average: -12%
  • Andritz AG (OTCPK:ADRZF) – 12-month forward PE relative deviation from its 10-year average: -23%
  • Krones AG (OTCPK:KRNNF) – 12-month forward PE relative deviation from its 10-year average: -27%

data.title: Seeking Alpha analysts share their insights about 2025 and the broader markets
data.description:
Traders and investors have closely observed the broader market averages climb throughout 2024, but what does the future hold for 2025? With only a few days left in this year's trading activity, many are now turning their attention to what lies ahead.

Below, you can find insights from several analysts at Seeking Alpha, offering their perspectives on the market outlook for 2025 and what investors should expect in the coming year.

SA analyst Christopher Yates outlined: “There is ample evidence to suggest 2025 will be a much more challenging year for equities than we have experienced over the past 24 months,” in The Calm Before The Storm.

Kirk Spano noted: “The S&P 500 is dangerously overvalued, with multiple valuation measures at historic highs, suggesting a potential correction or recession… In 2025, market conditions are uncertain with high risks; investors should focus on reacting to changes rather than sticking to rigid narratives,” in Overvaluation And Uncertainty Is A Path To Correction In 2025.

SA analyst Lawrence Fuller highlighted “Despite market pullbacks and corrections, the underlying economic fundamentals remain strong, presenting a buying opportunity in a bull market expected to last through 2025,” in Stocks Have Swung From Overbought To Oversold Seemingly Overnight.

Avi Gilburt stated: “I foresee a long-term correction in the S&P 500… For gold, I predict a parabolic rally to 3300-3400 by 2026, followed by a significant 5-8 year correction,” in 2025: Seeing Some Crazy Things For Gold And The Stock Market.

Victor Dergunov said “Despite potential near-term volatility and pullbacks, the SPX remains in a constructive uptrend, with a 2025 year-end target of around 7,000,” in It's All About The Fed Now.

The blue-chip Dow (DJI) last closed at 42,326.87 points, while the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) last settled at 5,872.16 points. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) last ended at 19,392.69 points.

For investors looking to further track the major market averages they can look towards the following ETFs: (NYSEARCA:DIA), (DDM), (UDOW), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (NYSEARCA:SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (NASDAQ:QQQ), (QQQM), (QLD), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).
